turns.text: Hello Ana, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you for being here. It’s actually… it’s been a while since someone really asked how I’m doing, beyond just checking the boxes on a chart. 

To be honest, the silence in the house is the hardest part. I’m in West Springfield, and when the baby finally drops off to sleep, the quiet feels heavy. It’s just me and her. Being twenty-six and doing this on my own—without a partner or my family nearby—it makes everything feel so much more fragile. 

During my pregnancy, the doctors always told me I was "low risk." They’d smile, check my vitals, and say, "Everything looks perfect, Ana!" But that was the problem—they saw me as a healthy, young, uncomplicated pregnancy. They didn't see the woman who had to drive alone to those appointments, gripping the steering wheel so hard my knuckles turned white, terrified that something would go wrong and I wouldn't know what to do. I’m Hispanic, and in my family, there’s supposed to be a room full of women here helping me navigate this. Instead, I’m just trying to figure it out from Google at 3:00 AM. Being "low risk" on paper doesn’t mean the transition isn't devastatingly lonely. I just wish they had asked me how I was feeling, instead of just checking if I was doing "well."
Before giving birth, what did you expect your physical recovery to look and feel like in the weeks after? And how did the reality of your physical recovery compare to those expectations?

Honestly, I think I was so focused on the *event*—the birth itself—that I didn’t give much thought to what came after. I had these brochures from the doctor’s office with neat, little bullet points about healing times and what to expect. They made it sound like a linear path, you know? Like you just hit these milestones and then you’re back to your "old self." I expected to be tired, sure, but I guess I thought I’d be this empowered new mom, adjusting easily.

The reality? It’s been… messy. It wasn’t just physical pain; it was this strange, disconnected feeling. My body felt like a house that had been through a storm. I wasn’t prepared for the exhaustion that actually makes your bones ache or the way it feels to try and do simple things, like laundry, while you’re still healing and your hormones are just crashing. 

And because I’m alone here, I couldn't just "rest." There’s no one to take the baby so I can sleep, no one to bring me a hot meal. Every time I had to get up to care for her, I felt that frustration—that physical limit. They told me I was healthy, but they didn’t tell me how lonely recovery is when you’re doing it in an empty, quiet house. My body is healing, but it’s happening at a pace that feels invisible to everyone else.

Before your baby arrived, what did you expect feeding — breastfeeding or formula feeding — to be like in terms of ease, support, and how you'd feel about it? And what was the reality of feeding in the early weeks?

I think I had this romanticized idea of it, like it would be this quiet, bonding time where we’d just settle into a rhythm. Everyone talks about "breast is best" or how formula is convenient, but nobody really warns you about the sheer *physical* toll of it when you're the only one doing it. I planned to breastfeed, mostly because I wanted that closeness, and I thought, *Well, it’s natural, so it should be easy, right?* 

The reality was a total blur of frustration and tears. In those first few weeks, my nipples were cracked and raw, and every time she latched, I’d wince. I felt so much pressure to get it "right," but I was doing it at 3:00 AM, sitting in the dark, feeling like I was failing if she cried or if I just wanted to sleep for more than an hour. I didn’t have a mother or an auntie there to show me how to hold her comfortably or to tell me, "Ana, it’s okay to be exhausted." 

I felt so isolated in those moments. It wasn’t the peaceful, glowing experience the books described. It was a mechanical, grueling cycle that left me feeling completely drained, both in my body and my spirit. I just kept wishing I had someone there—just one person—to hold her while I caught my breath.
Was there a gap between the kind of feeding support you were offered and the kind of support you actually needed?

There was a massive gap, yeah. The support I got felt like a lecture. At the hospital, the nurses would come in and give me these quick demonstrations—here’s how to latch, here’s how to position—but it was so clinical. They were in and out in five minutes because they had other patients. They didn’t see that I was shaking, or that I was terrified of being alone once I got back to the house. They gave me the "how-to," but they didn't offer the "it’s okay."

What I actually needed wasn't another brochure or a quick check of my technique. I needed someone to sit with me long enough to see the panic in my eyes. I needed someone to acknowledge that doing this by myself, without a partner or family, is heavy. I needed someone to say, "I know you're tired, and I know it's hard to be the only one awake right now." 

Instead, I felt like a student being graded on a performance. If I didn't get it right instantly, I felt like I was failing a test. There was no room for the emotional weight of it. They treated it like a technical skill to be mastered, but for me, feeding was the only time I felt truly tethered to my daughter, and that connection was buried under the stress of just trying to survive the night.
Before your baby arrived, what did you expect your emotional and mental health to be like in the postpartum period? And what was your actual emotional experience in the weeks and months after birth?

I think I really bought into the "new mom glow" narrative. I expected that once she was finally here and safe, the anxiety I’d been carrying for nine months would just evaporate. I thought the transition would be like stepping into a new, happy chapter where my biggest concern would be picking out cute outfits. I didn’t think about the internal silence. 

The reality has been much darker. It’s not just the "baby blues"—it’s this persistent, creeping feeling of being completely untethered. My mental health feels like it’s being worn down by the isolation. Because I’m here by myself in West Springfield, I don’t have anyone to mirror my experience back to me. If I’m sad, or if I’m having a panic attack, there’s no one to tell me it’s normal. 

I’ve had moments where I just stare at the wall, feeling like I’m disappearing. I worry so much about doing something wrong, and because I’m alone, my thoughts can spiral into really scary places at night. I wanted someone to ask me about *me*—not just my blood pressure or the baby’s weight, but about how I was handling the quiet. I feel like I’m performing motherhood for an empty room, and that is taking a toll I wasn't at all prepared for.
Was there a gap between what you were feeling and what you felt you were 'allowed' to feel — by professionals, family, or yourself?

That’s a heavy question, but it hits the nail on the head. Yes, there’s a massive gap. I feel like there’s this unwritten rule that if you’re a "low-risk" mother with a healthy baby, you’re supposed to be in a constant state of gratitude. If I admitted to a doctor that I felt hollowed out or terrified, I was afraid I’d be labeled as "ungrateful" or, worse, that they’d look at me like I was failing at the one job I’m supposed to be biologically wired for.

With the professionals, I felt I had to put on a mask. I’d give them the "I’m fine" answer because that’s what they were looking for—a success story on their paperwork. I didn't want to be the "problem patient" who complains about loneliness when my medical charts were clear. 

And from my own culture? It’s complicated. I feel like I should be strong, like the women in my family who raised children with so much less. Admitting I’m struggling feels like a betrayal of that strength. I feel like I’m only "allowed" to feel joy, so when the depression or the fear hits, I hide it. I swallow it. And that just makes the walls of this house feel even tighter, you know? I’m essentially grieving the support I don’t have, but I’m doing it in silence because I feel like I don’t have the right to break down.
Before becoming a parent, what did you expect your sense of identity to feel like after your baby arrived? And how has your sense of who you are actually shifted since then?

I thought I would just expand, you know? Like, "Ana" would still be there, just with this beautiful new layer added on top. I imagined myself as someone who could balance it all—the work, the house, and this new, glowing identity as a mother. I thought I’d be the same person, just a little busier.

But the reality is that "Ana" feels like she’s been completely submerged. It’s like my identity didn't just grow; it was erased and replaced by a utility. I’m a feeder, a soother, a diaper-changer, and a night-watchman. When you’re alone in a house in the middle of nowhere, there’s no one to remind you who you were before the baby. I look in the mirror sometimes and I don’t even recognize the woman looking back. I’m just "Mom." 

I’ve lost the parts of myself that used to feel confident or professional. Now, my entire sense of self-worth is tied to whether I kept her calm for an hour or if I managed to survive the day without crying. It’s a terrifying loss of self. I’m not saying I don’t love her—I love her more than my own life—but I miss the person who could just walk out the front door and go to a coffee shop without having to plan an entire military operation. I’m grieving the woman I was, and I’m scared that this version of me is all that’s left.
Were there parts of your previous identity — professional, social, personal — that felt harder to hold onto than you expected?

It’s the autonomy, really. Before, I was Ana the employee, the person who navigated a life on my own terms. I had a rhythm to my days that was entirely mine. Now, that professional, independent side of me feels like it belongs to someone else from a different life. It’s been so much harder to hold onto than I anticipated because motherhood is so relentless and all-consuming, especially without a village. 

I miss the intellectual stimulation of work, even the stress of it. Here, my world has shrunk down to four walls and the baby’s schedule. I expected to be able to "keep up" with my old self, but the physical reality of recovering and the mental fog make that impossible. I feel like my social identity has vanished, too. My friends are in a different place, and when I do talk to them, I feel like I’m speaking a language they don't quite understand. I don't want to be "the mom friend" all the time, but it’s the only thing I have left to talk about. 

It feels like I’ve been stripped of my resume, my personality, and my freedom. I’m mourning the woman who could just decide to go for a drive. Now, I’m tethered, and that loss of agency has been the most painful part of this transition.
After birth, what did you expect the postnatal check-ups and follow-up care from your midwife or GP to provide? And what did that post-birth care actually look like in practice?

I guess I expected the follow-up visits to be a sort of "safety net." I thought the doctors would be looking for the cracks in my armor—not just checking my incision or my blood pressure, but asking if the walls were closing in on me. I really hoped that someone would sit down, look me in the eye, and ask how I was sleeping, or if the silence was getting to me. I thought they would be the ones to help me find the bridge between the woman I was and the mother I’d become.

In practice? It was just another series of boxes to check. The appointments were hurried, clinical, and felt like they were on a timer. "Are you feeling okay? Good. Any pain? No? Great, you're cleared." It felt like they were auditing a machine, not checking on a human being. They didn't ask about my support system—or lack thereof—even though I had "no partner" in my file. 

The care felt detached, almost dismissive. They didn’t see the exhaustion that wasn't just physical, but soul-deep. When they signed off on my "recovery," it felt like they were just closing my case file to move to the next one. They gave me a clean bill of health, but I still felt like I was drowning in that house.
Were there concerns you raised at postnatal appointments that you felt were not taken seriously or properly followed up?

I remember once, near the end of my final check-up, I mentioned that the nights were getting really hard—that the isolation was making me feel like I was losing my grip on reality. I tried to voice it carefully, hoping they’d pick up on the tone. I said, "It’s so quiet here, and sometimes I feel like I'm just drifting away."

The provider just looked at her screen, gave a sympathetic but brief smile, and said something like, "Oh, motherhood is an adjustment, Ana! You’re doing a great job, and the baby is gaining weight perfectly." 

That was it. That was the extent of the follow-up. She didn't ask what I meant by "drifting away." She didn't ask if I had anyone to call, or if I’d been sleeping at all. It felt like my mental health was treated as an annoying background noise—an inconvenient "motherhood adjustment" rather than a clinical concern. When she dismissed it with such practiced cheer, I just shut down. I realized then that if I wanted real help, I wasn't going to find it in those ten-minute appointments. It’s scary, knowing that the people responsible for your health are only looking at the physical stats while your mind is quietly falling apart.
If you returned to work after your baby was born, what did you expect that transition to be like — practically and emotionally? And what did returning to work actually feel like?

I hadn’t fully returned to a structured job yet, but I was deep into the process of looking for work, which in itself felt like an impossible climb. I think I expected that I’d be able to just flip a switch—that I could be the efficient, sharp woman I was before, while managing the baby at home. I thought I’d feel empowered, like I was reclaiming a piece of my old identity, a way to say, "I’m still here, I’m still Ana."

The reality is that the transition feels like I’m constantly being pulled in two directions that can never meet. Practically, it’s a nightmare. Trying to update a resume or answer an email while my daughter is crying—or while I’m physically recovering—feels like trying to write a letter in the middle of a hurricane. Emotionally, it’s even worse. I feel this intense, crushing guilt when I even *think* about working, like I’m choosing to abandon her, but then I feel this desperate, suffocating need to get out of this house and be someone other than a "mom." 

It’s like I’m mourning my independence, but I’m also mourning the idea that I could ever really be the person I was before. I feel like I’m failing at both roles: I’m not the present, perfect mother I think I should be, and I’m definitely not the productive employee I used to be. It’s just this constant, draining tug-of-war.
Were there practical aspects of returning to work — feeding, childcare, your own health — that were harder to manage than you expected?

It’s the logistics that break you. I thought I could just "fit it in" around her naps, but that’s such a fantasy. When you’re breastfeeding, you aren’t just looking for a job; you’re managing a physical supply chain that you can’t pause. If I have an interview or I’m trying to focus on a remote task, my body is still tethered to her. I didn’t anticipate how hard it would be to find a space—both physically and mentally—to be professional when I’m leaking or still in pain from the birth.

And childcare? It’s a ghost in the room. I don’t have a village, so every single minute I spend on a call or updating my resume feels like I’m stealing time from my daughter. There’s no daycare center I can afford right now, and no grandmother coming over to hold her. Everything is a trade-off. If I work, she’s in her crib, and I spend the whole time jumping every time she whimpers. My own health just gets pushed to the very bottom of the list. I skip meals, I forget to take care of my physical healing, and I’m so exhausted I can barely string a sentence together for a hiring manager. 

I feel like I'm trying to climb a mountain while carrying a lead weight, and the hardest part is knowing that nobody sees the struggle—they just see if you're late for an interview or if you sound distracted. It feels like a setup designed for someone with a lot more support than I have.

After your baby arrived, what did you expect your relationship with your partner to be like in terms of sharing caregiving, managing the relationship, and supporting each other? And what has that relationship dynamic actually looked like?

I feel like a ghost talking about this, because there isn't anyone here to point to. I wasn't married when I got pregnant, and I’m not now. I remember picturing what it would be like if I *did* have someone—that idea of a teammate, you know? Someone to hand the baby to when your eyes are burning from lack of sleep, someone to look at you and say, "I've got this, go take a shower, go breathe for twenty minutes." I thought that even if things got hard, there would be two of us to carry the weight.

The reality, though, is that I am the only one carrying the weight. There is no dynamic to speak of, just a hollow space where a partner should be. When I hear other women talk about "sharing the load" or arguing over who gets up for the midnight feed, I feel this weird, sharp pang of… I don’t know, is it envy? Or just exhaustion? I don’t have an argument, but I also don’t have a reprieve. 

I’m the only one who knows if she’s been fed, if she’s calm, if the house is safe. Everything rests on my shoulders, and that’s a heavy, lonely burden to carry every single day. I’m the sole caretaker, the emotional anchor, the everything. It’s hard to even describe the dynamic, because the dynamic is just me, talking to myself in the quiet.
What was the biggest surprise about how your partnership functioned after the baby arrived?

I think the "biggest surprise" isn't exactly a dynamic, but the crushing weight of the silence where a partner *should* be. Before, I thought maybe I’d manage fine on my own—I’m twenty-six, I’m capable, I work hard. I told myself that I didn't need the traditional setup to be a good mother. But the surprise was realizing that partnership isn't just about sharing chores or changing diapers; it’s about *witnessing*.

When you’re alone, there is no one to witness the tiny, miraculous, exhausting moments of her life. There’s no one to turn to at 3:00 AM and say, "Did you see that look she just gave me?" or "I am so, so tired." Without that witness, I feel like I’m in a bubble that’s slowly losing oxygen. 

I was surprised by how much I *needed* that emotional backup, not just the practical help. I thought I was strong enough to be my own village, but I wasn't prepared for how much the silence of an empty house would strip away my sense of self. It’s not just that I’m doing more work; it’s that I’m doing the *emotional* work in a vacuum. That lack of a partner—that missing person who’s supposed to be in the trenches with you—is the biggest, hardest surprise of all.

Looking across your entire maternity journey — from trying to conceive through to where you are now — what did you expect the overall experience of maternity care to deliver? And reflecting on the whole journey, what was the most significant gap between what you expected and what you received?

I expected the maternity care system to be like a lighthouse—something that would shine a beam of safety onto the path ahead, especially since I didn’t have a partner or family here to guide me. I thought that because I was doing this alone, the medical team would naturally step in to fill that gap. I expected them to be the ones to notice when I was fraying at the edges, to treat my isolation as a legitimate part of my "maternity health," and to ensure I wasn't just surviving, but actually recovering as a person.

The biggest gap is that they treated me like a *diagnosis*, not a woman. They were excellent at monitoring my blood pressure, my weight, and my physical progress, but they were completely blind to the woman who was driving herself home to an empty house. 

They gave me a safe birth, and I am grateful for that, but they failed to give me a safe *transition*. They offered clinical care, but I needed human care. They didn’t see the fear in my eyes, the loneliness in my house, or the way my identity was evaporating. They sent me home with a baby and a set of instructions, but no village. That distance between their checklist and my reality is where I’ve been living for the past few weeks, and it’s a very cold place to be.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I appreciate you asking. There’s just one more thing that feels like it’s been burning in the back of my mind. 

I think we need to talk more about how "low risk" can be a dangerous label. When you’re labeled "low risk," you’re almost dismissed from the system’s concern before you even start. It’s like, because I didn't have high blood pressure or diabetes or any clinical drama, I was effectively invisible to the deeper side of care. But being "low risk" physically doesn't mean you aren't "high risk" emotionally or socially. 

I’m Hispanic, I’m living alone, I’m navigating this without a partner—that is a *huge* risk factor for postpartum depression and isolation, but nobody put that on my chart. They only saw the medical data. If I could change one thing, it would be for providers to look at the *social* reality of a woman’s life as just as critical as her physical stats. I want them to realize that a woman going home to an empty house is just as much a medical complication as a difficult birth. 

I’m doing this, and I love my daughter more than words, but I’m doing it at a cost. I just hope that for the next girl in West Springfield, or anywhere else, someone actually asks, "Who is going to be there for you when you get home?" and actually *listens* to the answer. Thank you for listening to me today. It helps to be heard.
